title: Inheritance and Lifetime Gifts — The Real Taxation Nobody Explains
description: Understanding income tax on inheritances and gifts in the US/UK: how it works, differences between regions, whether it's more beneficial to inherit or receive a gift during your lifetime, and how to minimize your tax liability legally.

segments.voice: When someone passes away and leaves an inheritance, or when a parent decides to gift a property to their children during their lifetime, one of the most misunderstood taxes comes into play: the tax on inheritances and gifts, according to tax law. This tax is managed by the tax authority, which means what you pay can vary greatly depending on where you live. In this video, we explain how it really works and how to plan for it to pay the minimum legally.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance donation tax law regional differences

segments.voice: The tax on inheritances and gifts: who manages it. Although there's a basic national regulation, the tax authority has broad competencies to establish reductions, bonuses, and its own rates. The result is a huge gap between regions. In some areas, children and spouses have bonuses of up to 99% on this tax, which in practice means almost no taxation. In other regions, the tax can be significant even between parents and children.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance tax regional differences bonuses

segments.voice: Who pays the inheritance tax? It's paid by the heir or legatee, not the deceased. And it's paid in the region where the deceased resided, not where the heir lives. If your parent resided in one state and you in another, you pay tax in the state where your parent resided. The deadline to file the tax is 6 months from the date of death, extendable for another 6 months by requesting an extension within the first 5 months. Failing to file on time generates automatic penalties.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance tax payer heir residence region 6 months deadline

segments.voice: The groups of relatives and their fiscal importance. The law divides heirs into four groups based on their relationship. Group I: descendants under 21, with the highest reduction. Group II: descendants over 21, spouses, and ancestors, with significant reductions. Group III: second and third-degree relatives, like siblings, nieces, and uncles. Group IV: fourth-degree relatives or more and individuals without a family relationship. The lower the group, the lower the relationship and the higher the taxation. Inheriting from an uncle can cost much more than inheriting from a parent.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance groups parentage I II III IV taxation levels

segments.voice: National reductions based on relationships. National regulations establish minimum reductions that regions can improve but not worsen. Group I, descendants under 21: 15,956 dollars plus 3,990 dollars for each year under 21 the heir is. Group II, descendants over 21 and spouses: 15,956 dollars. Group III: 7,993 dollars. Group IV: no national reduction. These amounts apply to the taxable base before calculating the tax. But many regions expand these reductions to make them virtually total.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance reduction national minimum parentage groups dollars amounts

segments.voice: The variation between regions: concrete examples. In some regions: a 99% bonus on the tax quota for descendants, ancestors, and spouses. Result: practically no tax. In other regions: the same 99% bonus. In some areas: a completely different tax regime, with lower rates for direct family members. In other areas: less generous reductions, the tax can represent between 5 and 20% of the inheritance for descendants in certain amounts. Before any estate planning, analyze the regime of the region where the future deceased resides.
segments.visual: US UK inheritance tax comparison regions bonuses

segments.voice: Inheriting the main home: the special reduction. In national regulations, there's a specific reduction of 95% for inheriting the main home of the deceased, with a limit of 122,606 dollars per heir. This reduction applies to spouses, descendants, and ancestors. The condition is that the heir must keep the home for at least 10 years; otherwise, they must return the saved tax. Many regions improve this reduction and reduce the maintenance period.
segments.visual: US UK main home inheritance 95 percent reduction keep 10 years

segments.voice: Is it more beneficial to inherit or receive a gift during your lifetime? This is the central strategic question in estate planning. Gifting the same property during your lifetime can be taxed differently. For gifting a property, the recipient pays the gift tax, and the donor may have a capital gain in their income tax if the property's value has increased since they bought it. This can make gifting during your lifetime more expensive than waiting for an inheritance.
segments.visual: US UK donation vs inheritance comparison lifetime gift tax planning

segments.voice: The donor's income tax on property gifts. When you gift a property to your children, from the donor's income tax perspective, the operation is taxed as if you had sold the property at its market value. If you bought the property for 100,000 dollars 20 years ago and it's now worth 200,000 dollars, the tax authority considers you have a capital gain of 100,000 dollars, even though you didn't receive any money. This gain is taxed in the savings base of the income tax at 19-27%. This is a critical point many families are unaware of and can generate an unexpected tax bill.
segments.visual: US UK donor income tax gift property capital gain tax problem unknown

segments.voice: The municipal capital gains tax on inheritances and gifts. Besides the inheritance tax and income tax, in property transfers, you must consider the municipal capital gains tax. In inheritances, it's paid by the heir. In gifts, it's paid by the recipient. After the Constitutional Court's ruling in 2021, the tax was reformed, and now there's the option to calculate the capital gains tax using the real or objective method, choosing the one that's more favorable.
segments.visual: US UK municipal capital gains tax inheritance donation 2021

segments.voice: Succession agreements in regions where they are allowed. In some regions, succession agreements allow the transfer of properties from parents to children during their lifetime with more favorable tax treatment. In some of these territories, the transfer through a succession agreement is exempt from municipal capital gains tax, and the donor's capital gain is not taxed in their income tax. These are very efficient tools in the regions that allow them but require a notarial deed.
segments.visual: US UK succession agreement regional law inheritance plan

segments.voice: Life insurance as a tool for estate planning. Life insurance capitals paid upon the death of the insured are taxed in the inheritance tax, not in the income tax. But they have a specific national reduction of 9,195 dollars per beneficiary of groups I and II. Additionally, in some regions, life insurance has additional bonuses. A well-structured life insurance policy can be an efficient way to transfer capital to beneficiaries with a lower tax burden.
segments.visual: US UK life insurance inheritance tax succession planning beneficiary

segments.voice: The usufruct as an instrument for planning. The surviving spouse has a legal right to the usufruct of the property. In fiscal terms, when the full ownership is divided into bare ownership for the children and usufruct for the spouse, each part is taxed according to its proportion. The usufruct is valued at 70% of the total value minus 1% for each year of age of the usufructuary. This division can reduce the total tax burden of the inheritance.
segments.visual: US UK usufruct inheritance tax split bare ownership strategy

segments.voice: Summary of basic estate planning. First: know the regulations of the region where you reside, which can be very different from national regulations. Second: if the main asset is a property, analyze the tax costs of both gifting during your lifetime and inheriting, including the donor's income tax. Third: in regions with 99% bonuses, inheriting is practically tax-free for children and spouses, making complex planning unnecessary. Fourth: consult with a notary and tax advisor for significant amounts; the cost of advice is always less than the possible savings.
segments.visual: US UK estate planning summary region notary advisor strategy
